title: Moong Dal Chilla (Lentil Savoury Pancakes)

subs.name: chana dal (soaked 4 hours) — richer and slightly denser, equally protein-rich
0.5 tsp ginger powder — works fine in the batter
zero oil on a well-seasoned cast-iron tawa — lowers calories further

steps.text: Wash the moong dal in two changes of water, then cover with fresh water and soak for at least 1 hour (overnight is fine). The dal will swell to nearly double its size — this is what makes the batter grind smoothly and the chilla light rather than dense.

Drain the soaked moong and transfer to a blender or mixie. Add ginger, green chilli, cumin seeds, turmeric, asafoetida and just enough water (3-4 tbsp) to grind into a smooth, thick batter. Season with salt. The batter should be slightly thicker than pancake batter — add water one tablespoon at a time if needed.
Heat a tawa on medium-high flame. Rub a few drops of oil across the surface with a folded kitchen cloth. Once the tawa is hot enough that water drops skitter and vanish, it is ready.

Pour a ladleful of batter onto the centre and spread quickly into a thin, even round of about 15 cm in diameter. Scatter a pinch of chopped onion and coriander on top if using. Drizzle a few drops of oil around the edges and cook for 2-3 minutes until the edges lift slightly and the surface turns from shiny to matte.

Flip with a wide flat spatula and cook the second side for 1-2 minutes until light golden spots appear. Remove and serve hot immediately with green chutney or a bowl of low-fat curd. Repeat for the remaining batter — makes 4-5 chillas.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.

substitution_notes: moong-dal → chana dal (soaked 4 hours) — richer and slightly denser, equally protein-rich; ginger → 0.5 tsp ginger powder — works fine in the batter; oil → zero oil on a well-seasoned cast-iron tawa — lowers calories further
